verses.text: A leper says, 'Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean'; Jesus touches him, 'I will; be thou clean.' Willing power. Christ both can and delights to heal the outcast.
A leper says, 'Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean'; Jesus touches him, 'I will; be thou clean.' Willing power. Christ both can and delights to heal the outcast.

A centurion seeks healing for his servant: 'speak the word only,' for he too is a man under authority. Faith that needs no sight. He trusts Christ's bare word to do the work.

Jesus marvels: 'I have not found so great faith... in Israel'; many shall come from east and west. Gentile faith. The outsider's trust shames the privileged.

He heals Peter's mother-in-law and many sick, fulfilling, 'Himself took our infirmities.' The burden-bearer. Christ's healings flow from His bearing our weakness.

A scribe offers to follow; Jesus warns, 'the Son of man hath not where to lay his head.' Count the cost. To follow Christ is to share His homelessness.

To one who would first bury his father, Jesus says, 'Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead.' Christ first. His claim comes before every other tie.
To one who would first bury his father, Jesus says, 'Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead.' Christ first. His claim comes before every other tie.
A storm rises; He rebukes the wind and sea, and there is 'a great calm'; the men marvel, 'What manner of man is this?' Lord of nature. Even wind and waves obey His word.

Two demoniacs meet Him; the devils beg to be sent into the swine. Powers in subjection. The very demons own His authority and plead before Him.
Two demoniacs meet Him; the devils beg to be sent into the swine. Powers in subjection. The very demons own His authority and plead before Him.

Two demoniacs meet Him; the devils beg to be sent into the swine. Powers in subjection. The very demons own His authority and plead before Him.
He says, Go; the swine rush into the sea; the city begs Him to depart. Loss preferred to Christ. Men would rather keep their pigs than welcome the Saviour.
